subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the sequence a1,a2,a3,…… such that a1=1,a2=2 and an+2=2an+1+an for n=1,2,3,…
If a1+1a2a3·a2+1a3a4·a3+1a4a5…a30+1a31a32=2αC3161 then α is equal to
question:
Consider the sequence a1,a2,a3,…… such that a1=1,a2=2 and an+2=2an+1+an for n=1,2,3,…
If a1+1a2a3·a2+1a3a4·a3+1a4a5…a30+1a31a32=2αC3161 then α is equal to
solution:
Given an+2=2an+1+an

 ⇒an+2an+1-anan+1=2

Now, Tr=ar+1ar is an A.P. with common difference 2

Now, T1=a1a2=2, T2=a2a3=4, ...., Tr=2r

So a1+1a2a3·a2+1a3a4⋯a30+1a31a32=∏i=130ai+1ai+1ai+2

=∏i=130aiai+1+1ai+1ai+2=∏i=130Tr+1Tr+1

=∏i=1302r+12r+2=3·5·7⋯614·6·8⋯62=1·2·3·4·5·6·7⋯61·6224·6·8⋯622

=62!24·6·8⋯622=62!261·31!2=6261!261·3131!30!

=2−60⋅61C30

=2-60·C3161

Now on comparing with 2αC3161

We get α=-60

subject: Mathematics
q: Number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-digit numbers that are less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2800</mn></math> and either divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> or by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> , is equal to _____ .
question: Number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math>-digit numbers that are less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2800</mn></math> and either divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> or by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> , is equal to _____ .
solution:
Four-digit numbers that are less than or equal to 2800 are 1000,1001,1002,....,2800

Numbers divisible by 3 are 1002, 1005, ....,2796,2799

Let total numbers be n, then

1002+n-1 3=2799 

⇒n=600

Numbers divisible by 11 are 1001, 1012, ..., 2794

Let total numbers be m, then

2794=1001+m-111

⇒m=164

Numbers divisible by 33 are

1023,1056,...,2772

Let total numbers be r, then

2772=1023+r-133

⇒r=54

Number of 4-digit numbers that are less than or equal to 2800 and either divisible by 3 or by 11 , is equal to

=600+164-54=710

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of an infinite geometric series with positive terms is 3 and the sum of the cubes of its terms is $\frac{27}{19}$. Then the common ratio of this series is:
question: The sum of an infinite geometric series with positive terms is 3 and the sum of the cubes of its terms is $\frac{27}{19}$. Then the common ratio of this series is:
solution:
Let the terms of infinite series are $a, a r, a r^{2}, a r^{3}, \ldots$

So, $\quad \frac{a}{1-r}=3$

Since, sum of cubes of its terms is $\frac{27}{19}$ that is sum of $a^{3}$,

$a^{3} r^{3}, \ldots \infty$ is $\frac{27}{19}$

So, $\frac{a^{3}}{1-r^{3}}=\frac{27}{19}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{a}{1-r} \times \frac{a^{2}}{\left(1+r^{2}+r\right)}=\frac{27}{19}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{9\left(1+r^{2}-2 r\right) \times 3}{1+r^{2}+r}=\frac{27}{19}$

$\Rightarrow \quad 6 r^{2}-13 r+6=0$

$\Rightarrow(3 r-2)(2 r-3)=0$

$\Rightarrow r=\frac{2}{3},$ or $\frac{3}{2}$

As $|r|  < 1$

So, $r=\frac{2}{3}$

q: The interior angles of a polygon with n sides, are in an A.P. with common difference $6^{\circ}$. If the largest interior angle of the polygon is $219^{\circ}$, then n is equal to
question: The interior angles of a polygon with n sides, are in an A.P. with common difference $6^{\circ}$. If the largest interior angle of the polygon is $219^{\circ}$, then n is equal to
solution:
$\frac{n}{2}[2 a+(n-1) 6]=(n-2) 180^{\circ}$
and $a n+3 n^2-3 n=3 n(n-2) 180^{\circ}$...(i)
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \text { Given } a+(n-1) 6^{\circ}=219^{\circ} \\
& \Rightarrow a=225^{\circ}-6 n^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$
Putting value of $a$ in (i)
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { We get }\left(225-6 n^2\right)+3 n^2-3 n=180 n-360^{\circ} \\
& \Rightarrow 2 n^2-42 n-360=0 \\
& \Rightarrow n^2-14 n-120=0 \\
& \Rightarrow(n-20)(n+6)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow n=20,-6 \text { (Rejected) } \\
& \therefore n=20
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A_n=\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)-\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^2+\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^3-\ldots+(-1)^{n-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^n$ and $B_n=1-A_n$. Then, the least odd natural number $p$, so that $B_n>A_n$, for all $n \geq p$ is
question: Let $A_n=\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)-\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^2+\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^3-\ldots+(-1)^{n-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^n$ and $B_n=1-A_n$. Then, the least odd natural number $p$, so that $B_n>A_n$, for all $n \geq p$ is
solution:
$$
A_n=\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)-\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^2+\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^3-\ldots+(-1)^{n-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^n
$$
Which is a G.P. with $a=\frac{3}{4}, r=\frac{-3}{4}$ and number of terms $=n$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore A_n &=\frac{\frac{3}{4} \times\left(1-\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^n\right)}{1-\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)}=\frac{\frac{3}{4} \times\left(1-\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^n\right)}{\frac{7}{4}} \\
\Rightarrow A_n=\frac{3}{7}\left[1-\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^n\right] \\
\text { As, } B_n=1-A_n
\end{aligned}
$$
For least odd natural number $\mathrm{p}$, such that $B_n>A_n$
$$
\Rightarrow 1-A_n>A_n \Rightarrow 1>2 \times A_n \Rightarrow A_n < \frac{1}{2}
$$
From eqn. (1), we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{3}{7} \times\left[1-\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^n\right] < \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow 1-\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^n < \frac{7}{6} \\
&\Rightarrow 1-\frac{7}{6} < \left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^n \Rightarrow \frac{-1}{6} < \left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^n
\end{aligned}
$$
As $n$ is odd, then $\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^n=-\frac{3^n}{4}$
So $\frac{-1}{6} < -\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^n \Rightarrow \frac{1}{6}>\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^n$
$$
\log \left(\frac{1}{6}\right)=n \log \left(\frac{3}{4}\right) \Rightarrow 6.228 < n
$$
Hence, $n$ should be 7 .

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <an> be a sequence such that a1+a2+...+an=n2+3nn+1n+2. If 28∑k=1101ak=p1 p2 p3 ... pm, where p1, p2, ... pm are the first m prime numbers, then m is equal to
question: Let <an> be a sequence such that a1+a2+...+an=n2+3nn+1n+2. If 28∑k=1101ak=p1 p2 p3 ... pm, where p1, p2, ... pm are the first m prime numbers, then m is equal to
solution:
Given,

Sn=∑i=1nai=n2+3nn+1n+2

Now we know that,

an=Sn-Sn-1

⇒an=n2+3nn+1n+2-n-12+3n-1nn+1

⇒an=4nn+1n+2

Now solving, ∑k=1101ak=14∑k=110kk+1k+2

⇒∑k=1101ak=116∑k=110kk+1k+2k+3-k-1kk+1k+2

⇒∑k=1101ak=1161·2·3·4.-0+2·3·4·5-1·2·3·4....+10·11·12·13-9·10·11·12

⇒∑k=1101ak=11610·11·12·13-0

⇒∑k=1101ak=125·11·3·13

⇒ 28∑k=1101ak=28×5×11×3×132

⇒28∑k=1101ak=2·3·5·7·11·13

Hence, there are six prime numbers in multiplications,

So, m=6

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the sum of the first 20 terms of the series
$\frac{4.1}{4+3.1^2+1^4}+\frac{4.2}{4+3.2^2+2^4}+\frac{4.3}{4+3.3^2+3^4}+\frac{4.4}{4+3.4^2+4^4}+\ldots$
is $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are coprime, then $m+n$ is equal to :-
question:
If the sum of the first 20 terms of the series
$\frac{4.1}{4+3.1^2+1^4}+\frac{4.2}{4+3.2^2+2^4}+\frac{4.3}{4+3.3^2+3^4}+\frac{4.4}{4+3.4^2+4^4}+\ldots$
is $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are coprime, then $m+n$ is equal to :-
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{20} \frac{4 \mathrm{r}}{4+3 \mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{r}^4} \\ & \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{20} \frac{4 \mathrm{r}}{\left(\mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{r}+2\right)\left(\mathrm{r}^2-\mathrm{r}+2\right)} \\ & 2 \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{20}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^2-\mathrm{r}+2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^2+\mathrm{r}+2}\right) \\ & 2\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}\right) \\ & \frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{8} \\ & \frac{1}{8}-\frac{1}{14} \\ & \left(\frac{1}{382}-\frac{1}{422}\right) \\ & =2\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{422}\right) \\ & =\frac{420}{422} \\ & =\frac{210}{211} \\ & \text { option }(3)\end{aligned}$

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>1</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>11</mn></msup></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>1</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>11</mn></msup></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is equal to

S'=210+29·3+28·32+…….+2·39+310

G.P. →a=1,r=32,n=11

S'=210·3211-132-1=211311211-1

=311-211

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of all those terms, of the arithmetic progression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>373</mn></math>, which are not divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, is equal to ________.
question: The sum of all those terms, of the arithmetic progression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>373</mn></math>, which are not divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, is equal to ________.
solution:
Given sequence is 3,8,13,.....373.

Here a=3, d=5 and an=373

Now an=a+n-1d

⇒373=3+n-15

⇒n=75

We know that Sn=n2a+an

⇒S75=7523+373

⇒S75=14100

Now let us write the sequence of terms which are divisible by 3.

We get, 3,18,33,.....363

⇒363=3+n-115

⇒n=25

Now let us find the sum of terms divisible by 3.

⇒Sdiv by 3=2523+363=4575

Required sum =Sum of 75 terms-Sum of terms divisible by 3

=14100-4575

=9525.

Therefore, the required sum is 9525

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let S be the sum of the first 9 term of the series :

x+ka+x2+k+2a+x3+k+4a+x4+k+6a+… where a≠0 and x ≠ 1. If S=x10-x+45ax-1x-1 , then k is equal to
question:
Let S be the sum of the first 9 term of the series :

x+ka+x2+k+2a+x3+k+4a+x4+k+6a+… where a≠0 and x ≠ 1. If S=x10-x+45ax-1x-1 , then k is equal to
solution:
S=x+ka+x2+k+2a+x3+k+4a+x4+k+6a+…

S=x+x2+x3+x4+...+ka+k+2a+k+4a+k+6a+…

S=xx9-1x-1+a·922k+82

xx9-1x-1+a·922k+82=x10-x+9·ak+8x-1x-1

So 45=9k+8⇒k=-3

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>c</mi></math> are in an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>3</mn><mi>c</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>3</mn><mi>c</mi><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>a</mi></math> are also in an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>c</mi></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>c</mi></math> are in an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>3</mn><mi>c</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mn>3</mn><mi>c</mi><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mi>a</mi></math> are also in an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>c</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given: loga, logb, logc are in A.P.

⇒2logb=loga+logc

⇒b2=ac   ...1

And loga-log2b, log2b-log3c, log3c-loga are in A.P 

⇒2log2b3c=loga2b+log3ca

⇒2b3c2=a2b×3ca

⇒2b3c2=3c2b

⇒2b=3c 

⇒4b2=9c2 & 6b=9c   ...2

Now solving the equation 1 & 2 we get,

4ac=9c2

⇒4a=9c   ...3

Then from equation 2 & 3 we get,

4a=6b=9c=k

⇒a=k4, b=k6 & c=k9

⇒a:b:c=14:16:19=9:6:4

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A B C$ be an equilateral triangle. A new triangle is formed by joining the middle points of all sides of the triangle $A B C$ and the same process is repeated infinitely many times. If $\mathrm{P}$ is the sum of perimeters and $Q$ is be the sum of areas of all the triangles formed in this process, then :
question: Let $A B C$ be an equilateral triangle. A new triangle is formed by joining the middle points of all sides of the triangle $A B C$ and the same process is repeated infinitely many times. If $\mathrm{P}$ is the sum of perimeters and $Q$ is be the sum of areas of all the triangles formed in this process, then :
solution: Area of first $\Delta=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{4}$ Area of second $\Delta=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{4} \frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{4}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{16}$ Area of third $\Delta=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{64}$ sum of area $=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{4}\left(1+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16} \ldots\right)$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}^2}{4} \frac{1}{\frac{3}{4}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \text { perimeter of } 1^{\text {st }} \Delta=3 \mathrm{a} \\ & \text { perimeter of } 2^{\text {nd }} \Delta=\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{2} \\ & \text { perimeter of } 3^{\text {rd }} \Delta=\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{4} \\ & \mathrm{P}=3 \mathrm{a}\left(1+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\ldots\right) \\ & \mathrm{P}=3 \mathrm{a} \cdot 2=6 \mathrm{a} \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{6} \\ & \mathrm{Q}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{P}^2}{36} \\ & \mathrm{P}^2=36 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{Q}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> be two distinct positive real numbers. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>11</mn><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> term of a GP, whose first term is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and third term is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math>, is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>p</mi><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> term of another GP, whose first term is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and fifth term is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> be two distinct positive real numbers. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>11</mn><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> term of a GP, whose first term is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and third term is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math>, is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>p</mi><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> term of another GP, whose first term is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and fifth term is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

1st GP⇒First term=a, a3=b

⇒ar2=b

⇒r2=ba

⇒a10=ar10=aba5   ...i

2nd GP⇒First term=a, a5=b

⇒aR4=b   ...ii

⇒ap=aRp-1

⇒ap=aba14p-1   ...ii

Now, using the given condition of pth term we get,

⇒aba5=abap-14

⇒ba5=bap-14

⇒5=p-14

⇒p=21

subject: Mathematics
q: Different A.P.'s are constructed with the first term <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math>, the last term <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>199</mn></math>, And integral common differences. The sum of the common differences of all such, A.P's having at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> terms and at most <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>33</mn></math> terms is.
question: Different A.P.'s are constructed with the first term <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math>, the last term <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>199</mn></math>, And integral common differences. The sum of the common differences of all such, A.P's having at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> terms and at most <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>33</mn></math> terms is.
solution:
Given 1st term of the A.P=100=a

And the last term =199=L

Let the three term be a,a+d,a+2d

an=L=a+n-1d

di=L-an-1

n→ number of terms

Now checking for what value of n we have integral common difference,

For, n=3, d1=199-1002

=992∉I

For, n=4, d2=993=33∈I

.

.

.

For, n=10, d3=999=11∈I

For, n=12, d4=9911=9∈I

So, sum of all common difference that are integer will be ∑di=33+11+9=53

q: If the <math><msup><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mn>9</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> terms of a non-constant arithmetic progression are in geometric progression, then the common ratio of this geometric progression is

Let the A.P. be a, a+d, a+2d, .....

d=a8

would be a+a8, a+a2, a+a

9a8, 3a2, 2a

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\left\langle a_{\mathrm{n}}\right\rangle$ be a sequence such that $a_0=0, a_1=\frac{1}{2}$ and $2 a_{\mathrm{n}+2}=5 a_{\mathrm{n}+1}-3 a_{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{n}=0,1,2,3, \ldots$. Then $\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{100} a_k$ is equal to
question: Let $\left\langle a_{\mathrm{n}}\right\rangle$ be a sequence such that $a_0=0, a_1=\frac{1}{2}$ and $2 a_{\mathrm{n}+2}=5 a_{\mathrm{n}+1}-3 a_{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{n}=0,1,2,3, \ldots$. Then $\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{100} a_k$ is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & a_0=0, a_1=\frac{1}{2} \\ & 2 a_{n+2}=5 a_{n+1}-3 a_n \\ & 2 x^2-5 x+3=0 \Rightarrow x=1,3 / 2 \\ & \therefore a_n=A l^n+B\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^n\end{aligned}$


$\left.\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{n}=0 & 0=\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \\ \mathrm{n}=1 & \frac{1}{2}=\mathrm{A}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~B}\end{array}\right] \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=-1 \\ & \mathrm{~B}=1\end{aligned}$


$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=-1+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{100} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{k}}=\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{100}(-1)+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{k}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =-100+\frac{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)\left(\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{100}-1\right)}{\frac{3}{2}-1} \\ & =-100+3\left(\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{100}-1\right) \\ & =3 \cdot\left(\mathrm{a}_{100}\right)-100\end{aligned}$

S2=1+6+11+16+21+…. is
question:
The 8th  common term of the series
S1=3+7+11+15+19+…

S2=1+6+11+16+21+…. is
solution:
Given,

Two A.P's  as

3,7,11,15,... 

First terma1=3

Common differenced1=4

And,

1,6,11,16,...,

First terma2=1

Common difference d2=5

Now, common difference of the series of the common terms of the given A.P's is d=LCMd1,d2=LCM4,5=20

Now, series of common term is 

11,31,51,71,...

So, 8th common term appearing in the both the series is

a8=11+8-1d

⇒a8=11+7×20=151

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the sum of the first $n$ terms of an A.P. be $3n^2 + 5n$. Then the sum of squares of the first $10$ terms of the A.P. is:
question: Let the sum of the first $n$ terms of an A.P. be $3n^2 + 5n$. Then the sum of squares of the first $10$ terms of the A.P. is:
solution:
Given the sum of the first $n$ terms of the A.P. is $S_n = 3n^2 + 5n$.

The $n$-th term of the A.P. is given by:

$T_n = S_n - S_{n-1}$

$T_n = (3n^2 + 5n) - [3(n-1)^2 + 5(n-1)]$

$T_n = 3n^2 + 5n - [3(n^2 - 2n + 1) + 5n - 5]$

$T_n = 3n^2 + 5n - (3n^2 - 6n + 3 + 5n - 5)$

$T_n = 6n + 2$

We need to find the sum of the squares of the first $10$ terms:

$\sum_{n=1}^{10} T_n^2 = \sum_{n=1}^{10} (6n + 2)^2$

$\sum_{n=1}^{10} T_n^2 = \sum_{n=1}^{10} (36n^2 + 24n + 4)$

$\sum_{n=1}^{10} T_n^2 = 36 \sum_{n=1}^{10} n^2 + 24 \sum_{n=1}^{10} n + \sum_{n=1}^{10} 4$

Using the formulas for the sum of squares and sum of first $n$ natural numbers:

$\sum_{n=1}^{10} T_n^2 = 36 \left( \dfrac{10 \times 11 \times 21}{6} \right) + 24 \left( \dfrac{10 \times 11}{2} \right) + 4 \times 10$

$\sum_{n=1}^{10} T_n^2 = 36(385) + 24(55) + 40$

$\sum_{n=1}^{10} T_n^2 = 13860 + 1320 + 40 = 15220$

Answer: $15220$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of 3 -digit numbers, that are divisible by 2 and 3 , but not divisible by 4 and 9 , is______.
question: The number of 3 -digit numbers, that are divisible by 2 and 3 , but not divisible by 4 and 9 , is______.
solution:
No, of 3 digits $=999-99=900$
No. of 3 digit numbers divisible by 2 & 3 i.e. by 6
$\frac{900}{6}=150$

No. of 3 digit numbers divisible by 4 & 9 i.e. by 36
$\frac{900}{36}=25$
$\therefore$ No of 3 digit numbers divisible by $2 ~\&~ 3$ but not by $4 ~\&~ 9$
$150-25=125$

q: Let s1,s2,s3....,s10 respectively be the sum of 12 terms of 10 A.Ps whose first terms are 1, 2, 3,....,10 and the common differences are 1, 3, 5,...,19 respectively. Then ∑i=110si is equal to
question: Let s1,s2,s3....,s10 respectively be the sum of 12 terms of 10 A.Ps whose first terms are 1, 2, 3,....,10 and the common differences are 1, 3, 5,...,19 respectively. Then ∑i=110si is equal to
solution:
Given,

First term of A.P are 1,2,3....10, so general term of the first term will be i

And common difference are 1,3,5,...., so general term of common difference is given by 2i-1

Now sum of the A.P  is given by,

Si=1222×i+12-12i-1

⇒Si=62×i+112i-1

⇒Si=144i-66

So, ∑i=110Si=∑i=110144i-66∑i=1101

⇒∑i=110Si=14410×112-66×10

⇒∑i=110Si=79210-66×10

⇒∑i=110Si=7260

Hence this is the correct option.

q:

If the $k^{\text {th }}$ row contains exactly $k$ numbers for every natural number $k$, then the row in which the number 5310 will be, is _______
question:
Let the positive integers be written in the form :
If the $k^{\text {th }}$ row contains exactly $k$ numbers for every natural number $k$, then the row in which the number 5310 will be, is _______
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{S}=1+2+4+7+\ldots \ldots+\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \mathrm{S}=1+2+4+\ldots \ldots \\ & \mathrm{Tn}=1+1+2+3+\ldots \ldots+\left(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}-1}\right) \\ & \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}-1}{2}\right)[2+(\mathrm{n}-2) \times 1] \\ & \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=1+1+\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)}{2} \\ & \mathrm{n}=100 \quad \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{n}}=1+\frac{100 \times 99}{2}=4950+1 \\ & \mathrm{n}=101 \quad \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{n}}=1+\frac{101 \times 100}{2}=5050+1=5051 \\ & \mathrm{n}=102 \quad \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{n}}=1+\frac{102 \times 101}{2}=5151+1=5152 \\ & \mathrm{n}=103 \quad \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{n}}=1+\frac{103 \times 102}{2}=5254 \\ & \mathrm{n}=104 \quad \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{n}}=1+\frac{104 \times 103}{2}=5357\end{aligned}$

q: If the first term of an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">.</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> and the sum of its first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> terms is equal to the sum of its next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> terms, then the common difference of this <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math>is
question: If the first term of an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">.</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> and the sum of its first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> terms is equal to the sum of its next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> terms, then the common difference of this <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math>is

⇒2×S25=S40

⇒2×2522×3+24d=4022×3+39d

⇒52×3+24d=42×3+39d

⇒30+120d=24+156d

⇒36d=6⇒d=16

q:

1100×101×102=k101, then 34k is equal to _______.
question:
If 12×3×4+13×4×5+14×5×6+…+
1100×101×102=k101, then 34k is equal to _______.
solution:
Given,

12.3.4+13.4.5+….+1100.101.102=k101

⇒22.3.4+23.4.5+….+2100.101.102=2k101

⇒4-22.3.4+5-33.4.5+…..+102-100100.101.102=2k101

⇒12×3-13×4+13×4-14×5+….+1100×101-1101×102=2k101

⇒12×3-1101×102=2k101

⇒1012×3-101101×102=2k

⇒2k=1016-1102

⇒34k=286

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi></math> be in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn></math> be in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, the arithmetic mean of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi></math> be in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn></math> be in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, the arithmetic mean of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is:
solution:
Given: 3, a, b, c are in A.P 

So, the common difference will be d=a-3

Now, b=2a-3 and c=3a-6

Also, 3, a-1, b+1, c+9 are in G.P

So, 3c+9=b+1a-1

⇒33a-6+9=2a-3+1a-1

⇒33a+3=2a-2a-1

⇒9a+9=2a2-4a+2

⇒2a2-13a-7=0

⇒a=7, -12rejected

So, b=2×7-3=11 & c=3×7-6=15

Now, the A.M of a, b, c is given by,

⇒A=a+b+c3

⇒A=7+11+153

⇒A=333

⇒A=11

subject: Mathematics
q: The value of $\sum_{k=1}^{10}\left(\sin \frac{2 k \pi}{11}+i \cos \frac{2 k \pi}{11}\right)$ is
question: The value of $\sum_{k=1}^{10}\left(\sin \frac{2 k \pi}{11}+i \cos \frac{2 k \pi}{11}\right)$ is
solution:
$\sum_{k=1}^{10}\left(\sin \frac{2 k \pi}{11}+i \cos \frac{2 k \pi}{11}\right)=\sum_{k=1}^{10} \sin \frac{2 k \pi}{11}+i \sum_{k=1}^{10} \cos \frac{2 k \pi}{11}$
$=0+i(-1)=-i$

question: If for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo></math>upto <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∞</mo></math> terms and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mrow><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
2+4+6+…+2y3+6+9+…+3y=4log10x

21+2+3+----y31+2+3+----y=4log10x

log10x=6

x=106

Now

y=log10x+log10x13+log10x19---∞

=1+13+19---∞log10x

=11-13log10x=9

So, x, y=106, 9
